title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
oneLiner: Acute infection of the upper female genital tract (endometritis, salpingitis, oophoritis, peritonitis) — often polymicrobial from ascending STI; missed PID causes infertility, ectopic, chronic pain.

sections.Killers First – What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** — massive haemoperitoneum. *Single finding: haemodynamic instability + positive β-hCG + unilateral adnexal mass.* Do not wait for ultrasound if unstable.
- **Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) rupture** — septic shock, peritonitis. *Single finding: severe diffuse rebound tenderness + high fever (≥39°C) + marked leucocytosis.* Needs urgent gynaecology review and IV antibiotics.
- **Sepsis from ascending infection** — especially if immunocompromised or IUD in situ. *Single finding: NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2, hypotension despite fluids.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3-5):** Bilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain (dull, constant), abnormal vaginal discharge (purulent/mucopurulent), intermenstrual or post-coital bleeding, deep dyspareunia, fever (≤38.5°C).
- **Key exam findings:** Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign on bimanual), adnexal tenderness (unilateral or bilateral), tender uterus, cervical mucopurulent discharge. Signs of peritonism (guarding, rebound) if severe or TOA.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
| Diagnosis | 1-line differentiator |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Ectopic pregnancy** | Positive β-hCG, unilateral adnexal mass, shoulder-tip pain if ruptured. |
| **Appendicitis** | Right iliac fossa pain, anorexia, vomiting, localised peritonism (Rovsing's sign). |
| **Ureteric colic** | Flank to groin colic, haematuria, renal angle tenderness. |
| **Ovarian cyst accident** (torsion/rupture) | Sudden severe unilateral pain, adnexal mass on exam/US, negative β-hCG. |
| **Endometriosis** | Cyclical pain, dysmenorrhoea, normal inflammatory markers. |
| **Urinary tract infection** | Dysuria, frequency, urine dip + nitrites/leucocytes. |
| **Splitter:** | No cervical motion tenderness → PID unlikely; test β-hCG + bimanual to separate ectopic vs. PID. |
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** Urine β-hCG (all reproductive-age women). Urine dip if dysuria. Check temperature, pulse, BP, oxygen saturations, NEWS2.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC), CRP, β-hCG, blood cultures if febrile/septic. STI screening: HIV, syphilis serology (consider later).
- **Imaging:** Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (first-line) – look for thickened/fluid-filled tubes, free fluid in Pouch of Douglas, TOA. Transabdominal if TVUS not tolerated. If TOA suspected, CT may be useful for surgical planning.
- **Special tests (microbiology):** Endocervical swab for *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* (NAAT), *Chlamydia trachomatis* (NAAT). High vaginal swab (HVS) for BV, trichomonas, candida. Consider rectal/oral swabs if history of anal/oral sex. **Never delay antibiotics for swab results in a clinically suspected case.**
- **Diagnostic criteria (CDC/NICE clinical criteria):** Minimum criteria: lower abdominal pain AND cervical motion tenderness AND/OR uterine/adnexal tenderness. Plus 1 or more: fever >38.3°C, mucopurulent discharge, elevated ESR/CRP, confirmed *N. gonorrhoeae* or *C. trachomatis* on swab. A definitive diagnosis is made on laparoscopy (rarely needed).
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine β-hCG** – missing ectopic pregnancy in a woman with PID symptoms is the single greatest error.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - Assess severity: NEWS2, signs of sepsis, peritonism, or haemodynamic instability → escalate.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO/IV, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600mg PO, if no contraindication).
  - Start empirical antibiotics within 1 hour of diagnosis (do not wait for swabs).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if: high fever, severe pain, vomiting, failed oral tolerance, suspected TOA, sepsis, pregnancy with PID, failure of oral therapy after 24h.
  - IV antibiotics regimen (severe): [check local policy] – commonly IV ceftriaxone 2g once daily + IV doxycycline 100mg BD (switch to oral when improving) + oral metronidazole 400mg BD (or IV if not tolerating PO). Duration: usually 14 days total.
  - Remove intrauterine device (IUD) if present **after antibiotics have been started** (NICE says consider removal if not improved after 72h; discuss with gynaecology). If IUD removed, do not insert a new one until infection resolved.
  - Pain management: regular paracetamol + NSAIDs, consider opiates if severe.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 14 days of antibiotics (oral doxycycline + metronidazole; ceftriaxone single IM/IV dose if given).
  - Screen and treat sexual partners (current and within 6 months) – refer to GUM clinic for contact tracing and STI testing.
  - Advise avoid sex until both patient and partner(s) have completed treatment (7 days after completing antibiotics and symptom resolution).
  - Arrange follow-up at 2 weeks: clinical review, repeat swabs if not improved.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1L/8h if nil by mouth, otherwise encourage oral fluids.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust if eGFR <30) + TED stockings.
  - Diet: light diet as tolerated; NBM if severe pain/peritonism → gynaecology review.
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then 4-6 hourly depending on severity.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (NICE 2019; UK guidelines):**
  - **Mild-moderate (outpatient):**
    - Ceftriaxone 500mg IM once (or IV if available) **PLUS** Doxycycline 100mg PO twice daily for 14 days **PLUS** Metronidazole 400mg PO twice daily for 14 days.
    - (Alternative single IM dose of ceftriaxone + azithromycin 1g PO once if doxycycline contraindicated – but NICE prefers doxycycline.)
  - **Severe (inpatient IV):**
    - Ceftriaxone 2g IV once daily (for at least 24h after clinical improvement, then switch to oral) **PLUS** Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV twice daily **PLUS** Metronidazole 400mg PO/IV twice daily (IV: 500mg TDS).
    - Duration: total 14 days.
- **Common alternative:** Ofloxacin 400mg PO BD + metronidazole 400mg PO BD for 14 days (if ceftriaxone/Doxycycline contraindicated – but not first-line due to resistance concerns).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: avoid in children <12 years and pregnancy (tetracyclines cause teeth/bone dysgenesis). Contraceptive pill efficacy reduced – advise barrier method.
  - Metronidazole: avoid alcohol (disulfiram reaction). Caution in hepatic impairment.
  - Ceftriaxone: caution if penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity rare but possible – use alternative e.g. cefoxitin? Check local).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment in renal impairment. Hepatic impairment: avoid or reduce (if severe).
  - Metronidazole: no change in renal impairment; in severe hepatic disease reduce dose (e.g. 50%).
  - Ceftriaxone: no renally adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting the third agent (metronidazole) in PID** – common mistake leads to failure to cover anaerobic bacteria, especially with IUD or TOA. Also: **Using doxycycline in pregnancy** (category D) – always check β-hCG first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) – risk of rupture, peritonitis, sepsis.
  - Fitz-Hugh–Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis) – RUQ pain, right shoulder pain, elevated LFTs.
  - Sepsis / bacteraemia.
  - Infertility from tubal damage (risk increases with each episode: 12% after first, 35% after third).
  - Ectopic pregnancy (7-10 fold increased risk).
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic pelvic pain (in 20-30%).
  - Dyspareunia.
  - Pelvic adhesions.
sections.Prognosis: - With prompt, appropriate antibiotics, >90% of uncomplicated PID resolves without sequelae. However, up to 1 in 5 women will develop infertility after a single episode; infertility risk is cumulative. Mortality is rare but occurs from ruptured TOA or sepsis.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always** test β-hCG before starting doxycycline in a woman of reproductive age – prevents tetracycline embryopathy.
- **Do** ask about IUD in situ – its removal (after starting antibiotics) is strongly associated with better outcomes in moderate-severe PID.
- **Do** prescribe the full 14-day course even if patient feels better at 5 days – incomplete treatment promotes recurrence and resistance.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not** delay antibiotics to collect swab results – clinical suspicion is enough. Start empiric therapy immediately.
- **Do not** prescribe azithromycin monotherapy for PID – it lacks anaerobic cover and has worse outcomes. Always combine with an anti-anaerobic (metronidazole).
- **Do not** discharge the patient without partner notification – untreated partners cause reinfection. Document that you have discussed GUM referral.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening abdominal pain, new fever/rigors, vomiting unable to keep oral antibiotics, heavy vaginal bleeding (suggesting ectopic or miscarriage).
- **Follow-up:** GUM clinic appointment within 2 weeks for partner notification and STI screen. GP clinic in 2-3 weeks for clinical review. Repeat swabs only if no improvement.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/gynaecology reg) if:**
  - Haemodynamic instability (BP <90/60) or tachycardia refractory to fluids.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising.
  - Suspected ruptured TOA (peritonism, rebound tenderness, high fever).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - Failure to improve after 48 hours of IV antibiotics (need imaging / surgical drainage).
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors.
  - GCS <13.
  - Airway compromise (rare, but consider Fitz-Hugh–Curtis causing diaphragmatic irritation precipitating respiratory distress).

rawMarkdown:
## Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
**One-liner:** Acute infection of the upper female genital tract (endometritis, salpingitis, oophoritis, peritonitis) — often polymicrobial from ascending STI; missed PID causes infertility, ectopic, chronic pain.

### Killers First – What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy** — massive haemoperitoneum. *Single finding: haemodynamic instability + positive β-hCG + unilateral adnexal mass.* Do not wait for ultrasound if unstable.
- **Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) rupture** — septic shock, peritonitis. *Single finding: severe diffuse rebound tenderness + high fever (≥39°C) + marked leucocytosis.* Needs urgent gynaecology review and IV antibiotics.
- **Sepsis from ascending infection** — especially if immunocompromised or IUD in situ. *Single finding: NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2, hypotension despite fluids.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (3-5):** Bilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain (dull, constant), abnormal vaginal discharge (purulent/mucopurulent), intermenstrual or post-coital bleeding, deep dyspareunia, fever (≤38.5°C).
- **Key exam findings:** Cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign on bimanual), adnexal tenderness (unilateral or bilateral), tender uterus, cervical mucopurulent discharge. Signs of peritonism (guarding, rebound) if severe or TOA.

### Differential Diagnosis
| Diagnosis | 1-line differentiator |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Ectopic pregnancy** | Positive β-hCG, unilateral adnexal mass, shoulder-tip pain if ruptured. |
| **Appendicitis** | Right iliac fossa pain, anorexia, vomiting, localised peritonism (Rovsing's sign). |
| **Ureteric colic** | Flank to groin colic, haematuria, renal angle tenderness. |
| **Ovarian cyst accident** (torsion/rupture) | Sudden severe unilateral pain, adnexal mass on exam/US, negative β-hCG. |
| **Endometriosis** | Cyclical pain, dysmenorrhoea, normal inflammatory markers. |
| **Urinary tract infection** | Dysuria, frequency, urine dip + nitrites/leucocytes. |
| **Splitter:** | No cervical motion tenderness → PID unlikely; test β-hCG + bimanual to separate ectopic vs. PID. |

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** Urine β-hCG (all reproductive-age women). Urine dip if dysuria. Check temperature, pulse, BP, oxygen saturations, NEWS2.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC), CRP, β-hCG, blood cultures if febrile/septic. STI screening: HIV, syphilis serology (consider later).
- **Imaging:** Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (first-line) – look for thickened/fluid-filled tubes, free fluid in Pouch of Douglas, TOA. Transabdominal if TVUS not tolerated. If TOA suspected, CT may be useful for surgical planning.
- **Special tests (microbiology):** Endocervical swab for *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* (NAAT), *Chlamydia trachomatis* (NAAT). High vaginal swab (HVS) for BV, trichomonas, candida. Consider rectal/oral swabs if history of anal/oral sex. **Never delay antibiotics for swab results in a clinically suspected case.**
- **Diagnostic criteria (CDC/NICE clinical criteria):** Minimum criteria: lower abdominal pain AND cervical motion tenderness AND/OR uterine/adnexal tenderness. Plus 1 or more: fever >38.3°C, mucopurulent discharge, elevated ESR/CRP, confirmed *N. gonorrhoeae* or *C. trachomatis* on swab. A definitive diagnosis is made on laparoscopy (rarely needed).
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine β-hCG** – missing ectopic pregnancy in a woman with PID symptoms is the single greatest error.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - Assess severity: NEWS2, signs of sepsis, peritonism, or haemodynamic instability → escalate.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO/IV, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600mg PO, if no contraindication).
  - Start empirical antibiotics within 1 hour of diagnosis (do not wait for swabs).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if: high fever, severe pain, vomiting, failed oral tolerance, suspected TOA, sepsis, pregnancy with PID, failure of oral therapy after 24h.
  - IV antibiotics regimen (severe): [check local policy] – commonly IV ceftriaxone 2g once daily + IV doxycycline 100mg BD (switch to oral when improving) + oral metronidazole 400mg BD (or IV if not tolerating PO). Duration: usually 14 days total.
  - Remove intrauterine device (IUD) if present **after antibiotics have been started** (NICE says consider removal if not improved after 72h; discuss with gynaecology). If IUD removed, do not insert a new one until infection resolved.
  - Pain management: regular paracetamol + NSAIDs, consider opiates if severe.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 14 days of antibiotics (oral doxycycline + metronidazole; ceftriaxone single IM/IV dose if given).
  - Screen and treat sexual partners (current and within 6 months) – refer to GUM clinic for contact tracing and STI testing.
  - Advise avoid sex until both patient and partner(s) have completed treatment (7 days after completing antibiotics and symptom resolution).
  - Arrange follow-up at 2 weeks: clinical review, repeat swabs if not improved.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 1L/8h if nil by mouth, otherwise encourage oral fluids.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust if eGFR <30) + TED stockings.
  - Diet: light diet as tolerated; NBM if severe pain/peritonism → gynaecology review.
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then 4-6 hourly depending on severity.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (NICE 2019; UK guidelines):**
  - **Mild-moderate (outpatient):**
    - Ceftriaxone 500mg IM once (or IV if available) **PLUS** Doxycycline 100mg PO twice daily for 14 days **PLUS** Metronidazole 400mg PO twice daily for 14 days.
    - (Alternative single IM dose of ceftriaxone + azithromycin 1g PO once if doxycycline contraindicated – but NICE prefers doxycycline.)
  - **Severe (inpatient IV):**
    - Ceftriaxone 2g IV once daily (for at least 24h after clinical improvement, then switch to oral) **PLUS** Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV twice daily **PLUS** Metronidazole 400mg PO/IV twice daily (IV: 500mg TDS).
    - Duration: total 14 days.
- **Common alternative:** Ofloxacin 400mg PO BD + metronidazole 400mg PO BD for 14 days (if ceftriaxone/Doxycycline contraindicated – but not first-line due to resistance concerns).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: avoid in children <12 years and pregnancy (tetracyclines cause teeth/bone dysgenesis). Contraceptive pill efficacy reduced – advise barrier method.
  - Metronidazole: avoid alcohol (disulfiram reaction). Caution in hepatic impairment.
  - Ceftriaxone: caution if penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity rare but possible – use alternative e.g. cefoxitin? Check local).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doxycycline: no dose adjustment in renal impairment. Hepatic impairment: avoid or reduce (if severe).
  - Metronidazole: no change in renal impairment; in severe hepatic disease reduce dose (e.g. 50%).
  - Ceftriaxone: no renally adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting the third agent (metronidazole) in PID** – common mistake leads to failure to cover anaerobic bacteria, especially with IUD or TOA. Also: **Using doxycycline in pregnancy** (category D) – always check β-hCG first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) – risk of rupture, peritonitis, sepsis.
  - Fitz-Hugh–Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis) – RUQ pain, right shoulder pain, elevated LFTs.
  - Sepsis / bacteraemia.
  - Infertility from tubal damage (risk increases with each episode: 12% after first, 35% after third).
  - Ectopic pregnancy (7-10 fold increased risk).
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic pelvic pain (in 20-30%).
  - Dyspareunia.
  - Pelvic adhesions.

### Prognosis
- With prompt, appropriate antibiotics, >90% of uncomplicated PID resolves without sequelae. However, up to 1 in 5 women will develop infertility after a single episode; infertility risk is cumulative. Mortality is rare but occurs from ruptured TOA or sepsis.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always** test β-hCG before starting doxycycline in a woman of reproductive age – prevents tetracycline embryopathy.
- **Do** ask about IUD in situ – its removal (after starting antibiotics) is strongly associated with better outcomes in moderate-severe PID.
- **Do** prescribe the full 14-day course even if patient feels better at 5 days – incomplete treatment promotes recurrence and resistance.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not** delay antibiotics to collect swab results – clinical suspicion is enough. Start empiric therapy immediately.
- **Do not** prescribe azithromycin monotherapy for PID – it lacks anaerobic cover and has worse outcomes. Always combine with an anti-anaerobic (metronidazole).
- **Do not** discharge the patient without partner notification – untreated partners cause reinfection. Document that you have discussed GUM referral.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening abdominal pain, new fever/rigors, vomiting unable to keep oral antibiotics, heavy vaginal bleeding (suggesting ectopic or miscarriage).
- **Follow-up:** GUM clinic appointment within 2 weeks for partner notification and STI screen. GP clinic in 2-3 weeks for clinical review. Repeat swabs only if no improvement.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/gynaecology reg) if:**
  - Haemodynamic instability (BP <90/60) or tachycardia refractory to fluids.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising.
  - Suspected ruptured TOA (peritonism, rebound tenderness, high fever).
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L.
  - Failure to improve after 48 hours of IV antibiotics (need imaging / surgical drainage).
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Septic shock requiring vasopressors.
  - GCS <13.
  - Airway compromise (rare, but consider Fitz-Hugh–Curtis causing diaphragmatic irritation precipitating respiratory distress).
